text: The purpose of this thematic issue is to map the diversity and institutionalisation of gender equality reforms within parliaments across the world. By bringing together a collection of articles based on case studies from both the Global North and South, the issue moves beyond the traditional discussions around the challenges and virtues of descriptive gender representation to focus on the challenges and opportunities related to building gender-responsive parliaments and institutions. Through a feminist institutionalist lens, the articles look into the dynamics and changes within parliamentary structures, processes, norms and policy outcomes and examine the ways in which parliaments are being re-constituted so as to address gender equality issues. It explores the roles and actions of critical masculinities and femininities, as well as the presence of coalitions to further the goals of gender equality, looking into the ways in which different local contexts of politics and cultures affect gender equality goals and how different factors influence the different levels of resistance to women's parliamentary empowerment (beyond the straightforward autocratic 'genderwashing' and the rise of the radical right, and into more everyday, familial, balance of care/work life issues). The issue also offers an innovative take on research methodologies and new theoretical frameworks, where some of the articles use the 'most significant change' methodologies, while others foreground the dynamics of gender within parliamentary groups and look into the parliamentary workplace as a gendered institution.
headline: Fully Human Written
